perturbation.original_text: If the Sponsor fails to pay ANTHEMIC within the designated time period, then interest will begin to accrue immediately on the past due amount at the rate of the lesser of the maximum amount allowed by law or 10% annually.
perturbation.changed_text: If the Sponsor fails to pay ANTHEMIC within the designated time period, then interest will begin to accrue immediately on the past due amount at a fixed rate of 15% annually.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text sets a fixed interest rate of 15% annually for late payments, regardless of what the maximum rate allowed by law is. This could lead to a contradiction if the legal maximum is less than 15% in California, where the agreement is governed. It creates uncertainty about which rate applies, potentially violating usury laws if the fixed rate exceeds the legal maximum.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Constitution Article XV Section 1 limits the interest rate for loans and forbearance to the greater of 10% per annum or 5% plus the discount rate of the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco. Setting a fixed rate of 15% regardless of this benchmark potentially violates California's usury laws if the legal maximum under Art. XV Section 1 is lower.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Codes Display Text skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Group Code Section Group Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ Add To My Favorites Code Text * California Constitution - CONS ARTICLE XV USURY [SECTION 1- SECTION 1.] ( Article 15 added June 8, 1976, by Prop. 14, clause 35. Res.Ch. 5, 1976. ) SECTION 1. The rate of interest upon the loan or forbearance of any money, goods, or things in action, or on accounts after demand, shall be 7 percent per annum but it shall be competent for the parties to any loan or forbearance of any money, goods or things in action to contract in writing for a rate of interest: (1) For any loan or forbearance of any money, goods, or things in action, if the money, goods, or things in action are for use primarily for personal, family, or household purposes, at a rate not exceeding 10 percent per annum; provided, however, that any loan or forbearance of any money, goods or things in action the proceeds of which are used primarily for the purchase, construction or improvement of real property shall not be deemed to be a use primarily for personal, family or household purposes; or (2) For any loan or forbearance of any money, goods, or things in action for any use other than specified in paragraph (1), at a rate not exceeding the higher of (a) 10 percent per annum or (b) 5 percent per annum plus the rate prevailing on the 25th day of the month preceding the earlier of (i) the date of execution of the contract to make the loan or forbearance, or (ii) the date of making the loan or forbearance established by the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco on advances to member banks under Sections 13 and 13a of the Federal Reserve Act as now in effect or hereafter from time to time amended (or if there is no such single determinable rate of advances, the closest counterpart of such rate as shall be designated by the Superintendent of Banks of the State of California unless some other person or agency is delegated such authority by the Legislature). No person, association, copartnership or corporation shall by charging any fee, bonus, commission, discount or other compensation receive from a borrower more than the interest authorized by this section upo
perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a legal contradiction by setting a fixed interest rate of 15% annually, potentially violating California's usury laws. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically Article XV Section 1 of the California Constitution, states that the interest rate shall be 7 percent per annum, but allows for a rate not exceeding 10 percent per annum for loans primarily for personal, family, or household purposes. The change removes the original text's safeguard of adhering to the 'lesser of the maximum amount allowed by law or 10% annually,' leading to a clear non-compliance if the legal maximum is less than 15%.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement is to be governed and construed according to the laws of the State of California without regard to conflicts of law.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement is to be governed and construed according to the laws of the State of Delaware without regard to conflicts of law.
perturbation.explanation: The original text specifies California law, while the modified text specifies Delaware law. This creates a direct conflict and introduces uncertainty because different states have different laws which could lead to different interpretations of the contract. Since both the agreement specifies governing law to be one of two different states, it can't be interpreted or enforced correctly.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Principles of Contract Law - Choice of Law
perturbation.law_citation: Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws Â§ 187
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification, while seemingly simple, creates a fundamental contradiction regarding which state's laws govern the interpretation and enforcement of the agreement. This directly contradicts the principles outlined in Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws Â§ 187, which governs choice-of-law clauses in contracts. The conflicting clauses make it impossible to determine which state's laws apply, leading to legal uncertainty and potential unenforceability of the agreement.

perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a direct legal contradiction by specifying two different states (California and Delaware) as the governing law of the contract. This directly contradicts the principle that a contract should have a clear and unambiguous choice of law, as highlighted in the Law Explanation's reference to Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws Â§ 187. While no law snippets directly confirm that contradiction, the core issue is that different states have different laws; therefore, specifying two different states introduces substantial legal uncertainty.
